challenges.title: Function with Return Value
challenges.description:
Write a C++ program that defines a function called 'add' which takes two int parameters and returns their sum. Call the function from main and display the result. Assign your C++ code to the variable `cpp`.

Your program must:
- Define a function named 'add' that takes two int parameters
- The function must return an int value
- Call the function from main
- Display the returned result with cout
challenges.test_code:
assert 'int add' in cpp, 'Should define a function named add that returns int'
assert 'return' in cpp, 'The function should return a value'
assert 'add(' in cpp, 'Should call the add function'
assert 'cout' in cpp, 'Should display the result with cout'
assert cpp.count('int') >= 3, 'Should have int return type and at least two int parameters'
hints: Define the function before main: int add(int a, int b) { return a + b; }
Call it from main: int result = add(5, 3);

challenges.title: Overloaded and Void Functions
challenges.description:
Write a C++ program that demonstrates function overloading by writing two functions with the same name 'display' but different parameter lists: one that takes an int and one that takes a string. Also write a void function called 'printLine' that prints a line of dashes. Assign your C++ code to the variable `cpp`.

Your program must:
- Define two overloaded functions named 'display' (one takes int, one takes string)
- Define a void function named 'printLine'
- Call all three functions from main
challenges.test_code:
assert cpp.count('display') >= 4, 'Should define two overloaded display functions and call them'
assert 'void printLine' in cpp or 'void printline' in cpp, 'Should define a void function named printLine'
assert 'void' in cpp, 'Should have at least one void function'
assert 'string' in cpp, 'One overload should take a string parameter'
assert 'int' in cpp, 'One overload should take an int parameter'
assert 'printLine(' in cpp or 'printline(' in cpp, 'Should call the printLine function'
hints: Overloading means same name, different parameters: void display(int n) and void display(string s)
A void function does not return a value: void printLine() { cout << "----------" << endl; }
